coredata.dc:description: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), a type of carbon material having size less than 10 nm and strong fluorescence is creating a new paradigm in the field of bioimaging and other biomedical applications. Owing to simple and low cost of synthesis, tunable and precise optical properties, high photostability and ease of surface functionalization with biological entities there is a huge potential in the field of CQDs based cell imaging applications. A thorough knowledge and understanding of synthesis methods and optical properties are crucial in translation of CQDs based bioimaging research from research lab to commercial purposes for societal benefit. Different methods of synthesis like electrochemical, laser ablation, microwave radiation, and hydrothermal and optical properties like photoluminescent (PL) property, quantum confinement effect, doping influence on optical properties of CQDs are covered in this review. Surface functionalization agents employed for different bio applications have been reviewed along with the current challenges. The applications of CQDs in bioimaging, biosensing and drug delivery along with the existing challenges and future prospects are discussed.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots: Synthesis methods, functionalization and biomedical applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), a type of carbon material having size less than 10 nm and strong fluorescence is creating a new paradigm in the field of bioimaging and other biomedical applications. Owing to simple and low cost of synthesis, tunable and precise optical properties, high photostability and ease of surface functionalization with biological entities there is a huge potential in the field of CQDs based cell imaging applications. A thorough knowledge and understanding of synthesis methods and optical properties are crucial in translation of CQDs based bioimaging research from research lab to commercial purposes for societal benefit. Different methods of synthesis like electrochemical, laser ablation, microwave radiation, and hydrothermal and optical properties like photoluminescent (PL) property, quantum confinement effect, doping influence on optical properties of CQDs are covered in this review. Surface functionalization agents employed for different bio applications have been reviewed along with the current challenges. The applications of CQDs in bioimaging, biosensing and drug delivery along with the existing challenges and future prospects are discussed.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The nanocomposites have been interests for molecular imaging, bio-sensors, and other biological applications. In 2013, a new trend arrived in the emerging carbon nanomaterials family; fluorescent carbon quantum dots CQDs have considerable attention due to their tunable size and unique optical properties [1]. CQDs are contemplating a type of zero-dimensional carbonic nanomaterial, compared to spherical, tubular, and are in graphene family nanomaterials [2]. Carbon dots (C-dot), also known as carbon quantum dots or graphene quantum dots possess chemical structure and physical properties similar to those of graphene oxide. They differ from graphene oxide in terms of size, being quasi spherical nanoparticles with diameter below 10 nm [3]. The origin of CQDs was accidental during single-walled carbon nanotubes synthesis which is further named carbon nanoparticles (CNPs). CQDs have outstanding physical properties including tunable photoluminescence, up-conversation fluorescence, improved surface Raman spectroscopy, and fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET) which is based on quantum effect and surface state [4]. Fig. 1 represents an overview of CQD advantages.

The small-sized CQDs have been achieved by carbonization, microwave oven irradiation synthesis methods by using renewable resources and tiny molecules. Depending on fluorescence intensity and variations in synthesis methods abundance of sensing protocols can be achieved. [5]. CQDs have been reported for a variety of biomedical applications [6], such as in food safety [7], bioimaging [8], biosensing [9], drug delivery [10], wound healing [11]. CQDs have other important applications too, such as photocatalysis [12], chemical sensing [13], gas sensing [14], energy storage [15], solar cells [16] and photodynamic therapy [17].

This review gives an outline of the fundamental properties of CQDs along with their top-down, and bottom-up approaches to synthesis. In addition in-vivo, invitro bioimaging followed by biosensing and drug delivery applications is mentioned. This review would offer valuable insights into the topic and explore more about CQDs.

The fluorescent CQD was discovered accidentally, during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes by Yan et al. in 2004 [18]. Another group of scientist's Sun et al. found the carbon nanoparticles from graphite powder using the laser ablation technique and named small carbon nanoparticles as carbon quantum dots [19]. CQD having various structures can be either graphite or amorphous. The size of CQD can be tuned by different parameters like temperature, pressure, precursor of synthesis, and time. Carbon is a substance with low water solubility and low fluorescence properties. On the other hand, CQDs have a small size, high luminescence, and high hydro solubility. This is because CQDs have a carboxylic group within their boundaries [20]. The special structure of CQDs controls its exclusive properties at the nanoscale [21]. In general, CQDs are less than 10 nm in size at least; all carbon materials at the nanoscale are referred to as carbon quantum dots. The CQDs have a spatial structure of sp2/sp3 with fluorescent emission as an innate characteristic [22]. Over the last few years, CQDs have attracted most chemists to research because of their distinct properties such as abundant in nature, high surface area, adaptable functionalization, non-cytotoxic, and exceedingly water-soluble.

Numerous methods have been proposed for preparing CQDs over the past decade, which can be broadly categorized into the top-down and bottom-up approaches [23], and they are shown in Fig. 2 . The following three problems arise during the preparation of CQDs: (i)carbonaceous accumulation during carbonization, which may be prevented by electrochemical synthesis, confined pyrolysis, or solution chemistry methods; (ii) size control and uniformity, which are important for manufacturing uniform CQDs. (iii) Solubility and selected applications may be influenced by surface properties during preparation or after treatment. Here, the most methods have been discussed for CQDs synthesis.

The electrochemical approach involves a nonselective “top-down” chemical cutting process of a carbon material such as graphite, carbon nanotube, or carbon fiber electrodes [24]. The CQDs obtained through the electrochemical method have unequal sizes and require further purification through filtration or chromatography to obtain monodispersed CQDs [25]. The preparation of carbon dots from o-phenylenediamine (OPD) through polymerization, carbonization, and passivation at an anode is shortly reported. Shortly, each OPD molecule is oxidized at the anode to remove electrons from its nitrogen atom to form a free radical. Then, dimers, trimers, and polymers are formed by reacting radicals with OPD molecules. The protonation of the amino group in OPD is negligible, and thus polymers are liable to possess ladder structures [26]. To obtain CQDs polymers are further carbonized and passivated. The NaCl, H2, and Cl2 were formed at the cathode and anode during the electrolysis of the solution, which plays role in CQD formation.

Another modified synthesis by the one-pot electrochemical method is done by Deng et.al published work on CQDs. In that work, they have fabricated CQDs by alcohol using three-electrode systems. A pair of platinum (Pt) sheets was used as working and counter electrodes. Calomel electrodes fixed to a lugging capillary acted as reference electrodes. The distance between electrodes was controlled with rubber plugs. This electrochemical carbonization of alcohol into very tiny particles offers a successful and facile method for the preparation of high-quality CQDs as shown in Fig. 3
[27].

Graphite rods can be used as electrodes to synthesize green CQDs in alcoholic NaOH solutions. A constant current was applied to electrodes at 50 mA using a DC power supply [28]. The mechanism behind electrochemical synthesis could be described as 0.1M NaOH/ EtOH electrolyte solution which forms C2H5ONa and OH− ions. A negative charge containing free OH− ions attract towards anode which does electrochemical cutting graphite as they show production of CQDs [29].

CQDs were synthesized through a simple, convenient electrochemical method using sodium citrate and urea with appropriate proportion added into ultrapure water. Approximately 1h was spent at 5V DC until the color of the solution changed to brown. The brown solution was further purified by dialysis membrane to obtain fluorescent CQDs [30].

To obtain the colloidal solution of nanoparticles in a variety of solvents laser ablation method is used. Laser ablation provides high fabrication rates, fine particle size, and high monodispersity. The laser tuning and multi-target precursors are parameters that were used to control material properties through the laser ablation method. N-heptane was used as a laser ablation medium. 5mg of activated carbon was dispersed in 10ml of N-heptane using ultrasonication for 20 min. Followed by, laser irradiation for 130 min. The color of the suspension was changed to yellowish, which confirms the formation of CQDs as represented in Fig. 4 . The results indicated that activated carbon CQDs were promising candidates for designing large optical no liability devices for other photonic applications [31]. It is a simple, fast, and eco-friendly approach to synthesizing various nanomaterials including Green carbon dots (GCQDs) [32], carbon nanodots [33], metal nanoparticles [34], and transition metal dichalcogenides quantum dots [35]. Dispersed powders have many advantages over those dispersed in a solution, such as homogeneous surface structure and small size distribution. During laser ablation, complex functionalization of nanoparticles can be obtained in one step by adding selected ligands in different liquids. And active species can interact with molecules in liquid [36]. The method for synthesis by laser ablation is proposed in that boron nitrite was used as a precursor for CQD synthesis. The photoluminescence of boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) was tunable from UV to the green region by the femtosecond laser ablation method for the first time. The prepared CQDs emit three different types of luminescence ultraviolet, blue, and green using hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) powder in ethanol, diethylamine, and ethylenediamine. These BNCQDs may be used as fluorescence probes for bioimaging [37].

The combination of polyethyleneimine (PEI) and graphite was made fluorescent carbon dots by laser ablation. Kaczmarek et al. proposed nanoparticles or quasi molecular fluorophores which are a source of luminescence using PEI and ethylenediamine (EDA) The result shows that a 5-fold higher quantum yield was observed in dialysate than in retentate. This specifies the presence of a free fluorescent molecule [38].

Several synthesis methods have been published. Though, most of these are time-consuming, complex, and economically prohibitive [39,40]. Quite the reverse microwave-assisted synthesis can step up chemical reactions at low temperatures, within 100W-200W power. The rapid synthesis of nitrogen carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) at low temperature was developed using a 10 wt% glucose water solution mixed with ammonia hydroxide 25% solution. The mixed 5:1 proportion of glucose solution and ammonium hydroxide solution was kept for 1min in the microwave at 100°c. As prepared CQDs are had the highest percentage of nitrogen in the form of pyridinic/NH2 and are also used for photocatalytic degradation of Rose Bengal (RB). RB is an organic dye that is extremely gene toxic, mutagenic, and cytotoxic [41]. Moreover, another synthesis method was proposed utilizing Silk fibroin (SF) which is a biomedical waste. This method employs for bioimaging, biosensing, and drug delivery applications. Some other methods were also proposed by Li et al. using nitrogen-doped polymer like CQDs using cocoon silk by autoclave process [42]. But the microwave-assisted method is a more convenient technique having time reduction and also getting a more prominent size and focusing applications.

As stated by the literature, some possible ways to improve reaction using microwave-assisted technique [43] are as follows: (i) thermal effects [44] (ii) specific microwave effects, and (iii) non-thermal effects [45]. The lemon and onion biomass was used firstly to synthesize CQDs by microwave method as showed in Fig. 5 . This analytical method develops to determine commercially vitamin, and mineral supplements [46]. This proposed method quantifies riboflavin. Such quantification was compared with other reported methods [47,48] indicating that it may be used for the determination of riboflavin.

A hydrothermal synthesis is a bottom-up approach for preparing nanomaterials. This method is based on the solution reaction approach. In hydrothermal synthesis, a very high-temperature range is applied to reaction material in a Teflon-lined autoclave. In Teflon-lined autoclave pressure is created by tightly closing the valves [49]. The CQDs are synthesized by single-step pyrolysis using citric acid and branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) above 200°c vapor pressure. The PEI functionalized CQD with a quantum yield of 42.5% has been for chemical sensing [50].

CQDs synthesized through carrot juice by hydrothermal method demonstrated maximum PL excitation at a wavelength of 360nm showing emission at 442nm, revealing a general stroke shift of 82nm [51]. The emission peak wavelength depends on excitation wavelength because of the wide size distribution of CQDs [52]. A facile one-step approach has been applied for preparing CQDs from L-Lysine and thiourea. This synthesis gives nitrogen and sulfur-doped CQDs which have a high quantum yield of 53.19% and emit strong blue fluorescence under UV light and are applied for the detection of picric acid [53]. In literature, several studies were done using small organic molecules as the carbon source for the synthesis of CQDs. Zhang et al. for example, used carbon tetrachloride as a carbon source to make nitrogen-doped CQDs with NaNH2 as a nitrogen source via the hydrothermal method. Accordingly, the remarkable results show the size of prepared CQDs to be 3.3 nm with 0.5-5 nm height. This synthesis provides a graphite-like structure [54]. In another work in hydrothermal assisted pyrolysis synthesis Kundu et al. used a green route using citric acid and passivated with branched PEI to enhance surface functionality. As result, nitrogen-doped CQDs show multicolor emission and are applied for cellular imaging [55].

Xie et al. hydrothermally synthesized green CQDs from highland barley as carbon source and ethylenediamine as nitrogen source. These synthesized CQDs were used for Hg2+ detection by sensitivity and selectivity detected quenching. This method gives excitation wavelength-dependent characteristics at 4.5- 7 nm size and strong blue fluorescence [56]. Fig. 6 represents hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs using 1, 5-diaminonaphthalene (DAN) and citric acid along with structure of CQDs. Table 1 gives brief information about precursors used for synthesis and applications for the same.

While the optical properties of CQDs have attracted major attention, the origin of tunable PL remains a source of row with the deepening of research. Due to their nanoscale size, the nonzero band gap and related PL properties of CQDs are expected to be readily adjusted by changing the carbon core and surface chemistry. Two accepted mechanism models for the origin of luminescence of CQDs have been proposed: i. the band gap emission based on the conjugated π-domains, in which the quantum confinement effect is significant, and ii. The other involves the surface defect-states, mainly apparent as the surface effect [92]. CQDs typically show strong optical absorption in the UV region (230–320 nm), with a tail extending into the visible range. For the carbon core, a maximum peak at 230 nm is ascribed to the π–π* transition of aromatic C–C bonds, whereas a shoulder at 300 nm is attributed to the n–π* transition of C=O bonds or other connected groups [93]. In addition, the connected chemical groups may contribute to the absorption at the UV–visible regions. The observed deviations in absorption spectra data, at least to some extent, indicate differences in the compositions or structures of different hybridization derivatives. The fluorescence emission of CQDs is fairly complex and not fully understood. Conventionally, CQDs were analyzed at fixed excitation which was pH-dependent PL. Many other expectations were completely ignored [94–96], but still, another method was prepared by Dagar et al. using the above information proposed a new photoluminescence mechanism observed under different pH for a wide range of excitation. This gives further valuable information than traditional techniques [97]. This was excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior applied for multicolor imaging. Other results from Wang et al. research demonstrated that the emission peak is always allied to the excitation wavelength. This phenomenon is called wavelength-dependent behaviors. This can result from wide distributions of diverse changes in sizes of CQDs, surface chemistry, different emissive wavelength, or a mechanism that are currently doubted [98]. Fig. 7 depicts excitation-dependent photoluminescence in CQDs.

Excitation independent PL behavior studied by Dung Mai et al. using a mixture of citric acid and thiourea by solvothermal method gives solid films of CQDs which exhibited blue-green emission and red emission which was found to be independent of excitation wavelength [99]. Different data analysis techniques have been reported to measure PL. This can be useful for handling databases and analyzing the hyper spectral PL data set CQDs. These techniques are principal component analysis (PCA), partial least square analysis (PLS), hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA), non-negative matrix factorization (NMF), and multivariate curve resolution (MCR-ALS) [100].

Up-conversion luminescence of CQDs is an optical process that emits high-energy photons by absorbing two or more low-energy photons, which is caused by anti-Stokes luminescence, has attracted significant attention in recent years. Up-conversion PL of CQDs has been reported for two-photon absorption and anti-Stokes PL [101], it is relatively important to establish a proper characterization system in order to investigate these properties because some so-called “up-conversion PL” in CQDs could be due to the excitation of second-order diffraction light (wavelength λ/2) from the monochromators in the fluorescence spectrophotometer [102]. Amplified spontaneous green emission and lasing emission has also been observed from CNDs [103], and the high PL quantum yield and small overlap between the absorption and emission of CQDs in an ethanol solution were the key factors in achieving lasing emission. These special optical properties may lead to novel application of different types of CQDs. Deng et al. has proposed visible−ultraviolet up-conversion carbon quantum dots by one-step hydrothermal method, used for visible light catalytic ability of TiO2. This work broadened the application of up-conversion materials and improved the utilization efficiency of the light source [104]. CQDs with down and up-conversion PL properties had been synthesized through low-temperature carbonization in a facile one step green method from cabbage [105].

The particle size of the CQDs is smaller than de Broglie wavelength of the electron in the dot, creating a deviation from bulk properties. This leads to the quantum confinement effect. This confinement effect creates a quantization of the energy into discrete levels in the conduction and valence bands, so the C-dot can be implicit as a “virtual atom”. The emission energy depends on the radius of the particle so that as the particle gets smaller, both the excitation and emission spectrum shift to shorter wavelengths. In a distribution of CQDs with varying sizes, different subsets get favorably, excited as the excitation wavelength varies. As CQDs of different size are excited, the emission spectrum shifts with excitation wavelength [106].

The size confinement effect is the effect of spatial confinement of excited ions in one more dimension within a material and also electronic energy levels are discrete. Arpan et al. proposed size-dependent PL quenching of CQDs by Fe3+ ions of ferritin. The smaller size of CQDs observed at about < 2nm that easily penetrate ferritin shell and bigger size CQDs 4–6 nm remain outside at surface. This PL dependent inhomogeneous size distribution affects the application mechanism [107]. Changing parameters, synthesis methods, and changes in stroke shift affect the quantum confinement effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Doping influence on optical properties of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The emission spectra of CQDs can be tuned by doping with some elements such as nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), phosphorus (P), boron (B), or their combinations [108–112]. Heteroatom doping is a highly efficient and convenient approach to tune the optical properties and passivate the surface of CQDs. Heteroatom doped CQDs have attracted great attention and developed fast in recent years for simple preparation process and novel optical performance. It has been shown that doping of CQDs leads to the fluorescence enhancement and shift of emission spectra. Different emission centers and traps can be created with the introduction of doping atoms into CQDs. As a result, the emission spectra can be tuned to blue and even red spectra [113]. However, in some cases doping elements are located on the surface of CQDs only [114]. Thus, the competition between the optical centers, surface states, and traps is mainly responsible for the emission spectra of doped CQDs. The exact mechanism of fluorescence shifting in doped CQDs is still under research thus, further discoveries are expected in near future. Nitrogen-doped CQDs have been commonly utilized for fluorescent detection of pyridine [115], dopamine [116], amoxicillin [117], microRNA [118]. Future studies should be concentrated on the following open issues: (a) the origin of fluorescence shifting in doped CQDs; (b) the actual location of doped elements in CQDs; and (c) the role of doped elements in fluorescence sensing.

Surface passivation is the phenomenon that enhances the fluorescent as well as physical properties of CQDs. Surface functionalization possesses characteristics such as good conductivity, good photoluminescence, improved chemical composition, and thermal stability [119]. CQDs structurally contain sp2 and sp3 carbon domains and along with a large number of defects in sp2 and sp3 domains generate energy gaps that affect photoluminescence Therefore surface passivation is an important step to enhance PL properties and adsorption of biomolecules to CQDs [120].

Ren et al. have engineered p-phenylenediamine surface-modified CQD into silk fibroin nanofibres bioactive scaffolds with improved physicochemical and mechanical properties. This functionalization provides affinity towards the binding of CQD and scaffold to visualize cytotoxicity to cardiomyocytes [121].

Various polymers that have been employed for surface functionalization such as polyethylene glycol (PEG) [122] and polyethyleneimine PEI [123]. This indicates that functionalization agents play important role in improving PL properties. Using this phenomenon nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, and phosphorous groups have also been doped [124].

The CQDs obtained from different precursors have different structures either graphitic or amorphous. The size of nanomaterials can be tuned by different nanocomposites. The Fig. 8 showed analytical techniques used to characterize CQDs such as UV- vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), x-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) [125–128].

The size distribution of CQDs is statistically analyzed by using TEM and AFM typically small between 1 and 10 nm [129]. The basic composition of CQD consists of C, H, O, and N elements and it is confirmed by XPS and EDX. XRD patterns demonstrated amorphous/ crystalline structure of CQD. The functional groups present on CQDs are C-O-C, COOH, C-OH, C-H, and C-C confirmed by FTIR. UV-vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to characterize the optical properties of CQDs. The optical properties including absorption, emission, and excitation peak was observed at 280–451 nm, 340 nm, and 480 nm in range, respectively [130].

CQDs are emerging nanomaterials with immense potential in bioimaging applications. The CQDs possess superior optical properties including high brightness, spontaneous penetration, and great biocompatibility to owe their bioimaging applications. The challenges are being faced by researchers to fabricate CQDs with improved diagnostic and therapeutic approaches [131]. On the other hand, diverse types of cells or tissues having special structures corresponding to these nanoparticles gives specific response to biomarkers on the cell membrane or nucleus membrane [132]. According to this, functionalized CQDs are developed to interact with proteins, lipids, targeting ligands, and biomarkers of different cells in imaging applications [133]. Fig. 9 represents the biomedical applications of CQDs overall.

In-vitro bioimaging focuses on cancer cell imaging, stem cell imaging, and neuron imaging. In-vivo imaging has been explored for tracking development, diagnosis, and treatment of cancers and neurological diseases [134]. One more biomedical application of CQDs in imaging is nucleus imaging, mitochondria, and endoplasmic reticulum imaging [135,136].

In-vitro and In-vivo bioimaging: The world is threatened with cancer, So many people die from cancer every year, early diagnosis and prevention of malignant tumors is enormously important. Currently, CQDs can penetrate a diverse of cancer cells, due to stable optical properties and biocompatibility of CQDs, cancer cells can be detected by fluorescence imaging of CQDs. Yue et al. has proposed ruthenium containing CQDs synthesis via a hydrothermal method for imaging HeLa cancer cells (Fig. 10 a) and zebrafish (10b) and cancer photodynamic therapy. The properties extreme water solubility, enhanced fluorescence quantum yield added in achieving forward applications [137]. Stem cell imaging is urgently needed because stem cells undergo the natural development process of organs and function as regeneration of bone, skin, and neuron repair. So, CQDs can easily penetrate stem cells by endocytosis mechanism.

Cancer stem cell-penetrating red carbon quantum dots (CSCNP-RCQDs) doxorubicin-loaded used for imaging as well as drug delivery into the nucleus of cancer stem cells. The internalization of doxorubicin into cancer cells results in CSCNP-RCQDs for the treatment of tumors. The ex vivo fluorescence images of isolated organs, including tumors, liver, kidney, lung, and brain were employed to evaluate the biodistribution of intravenously injected CSCNP-RCQDs. The images were captured using laser confocal scanning microscopy (LCSM) at an excitation wavelength of 561 nm (Fig. 11 a) and for in-vivo imaging, CSCNP-RCQDs were injected into tumor bearing nude mice. Fluorescence signals of mice were recorded within different time intervals (Fig. 11b) [138]. In another research, mesenchymal stem cells imaging was reported by Hao Chen et al. by using Gadolinium functionalized carbon dots (Gd-CDs). The Gd-CDs promotes the proliferation of human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs), which is tracked by the fluorescence resonance dual-modality imaging of hMSCs [139]. The human mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs), plays important role in tropism toward a injury site and is a promising candidate for regenerative medicine, targeted therapy, or treating tissues. These MSCs were visualized by quaternized CQDs as stem cell tracking probe for in vivo fluorescence imaging. This is first time reported study for safe application of adipose tissue derived MSCs in tumor bearing mouse. These quaternized CQDs were highly biocompatible even at highest concentration. Furthermore at lowest concentration quaternized CQDs did not cause any negative effect on native function and behavior of the cells [140].

AS1411 aptamer modified carbon dots via polyethylenimine (CDs‐PEI‐AS1411) nanosystem is efficient strategy for targeted cancer cell imaging. The CDs‐PEI‐AS1411 nanosystem in MCF-7 cells was significantly higher than that of mouse fibroblast cell line (L929), which revealed the highly selective detection ability of nucleolin‐positive cells. An efficient detecting and targeting nanosystem has been reported based on the DNA aptamer AS1411 modified CDs with polyethyleneimine (PEI) as connecting bridge [141].

Metal doped carbon quantum dots (M-CQDs) were utilized for neuron imaging and neuronal interactions. The effect of metal-doped CQDs on viability of PC12 neuronal cells was shown also, imaging was done without M-CQDs and with M-CQDs to compare or transfection into cells [142]. Nitrogen doped CQDs synthesized using Kraft lignin waste has been used for the brain cell (N27) imaging application [143]. Firstly reported cholera toxin B-carbon dots conjugate (CTB-CDs) were utilized as fluorescent retrograde tracer. Retrograde neuroanatomical tracing method is a key technique to study the complex interconnections of the nervous system. Traditional tracers have several drawbacks, including time-consuming immunohistochemical or immunofluorescent staining procedures, rapid fluorescence quenching and low fluorescence intensity [144].

Biosensing is the detection of target molecules based on immune system. Specificity and sensitivity are the main parameters for biosensing. The target molecule is labeled by the commonly requisite method to ensure specificity [145]. The CQDs have not only water solubility but also strongly interact with metal ions. The contribution of CQDs to detect metal ions depend on PL intensity such as Ca2+ [146], Fe3+ [147], Hg2+ [148], Fe2+ [149], Co2+ [150], Ni2+ [151], Al3+ [152], Cu2+ [153], Pb2+ [154] and Zn2+ [155]. Tetraphenylporphyrin (TPP) precursor and metal-doped CQDs were synthesized by Fengshou et al. for biosensing purposes. Based on a fluorescence detection sensor, Fe3+ ions were detected [156]. To detect hypochlorite (ClO−), a dual emission nanoprobe was used. The fluorescein surface-functionalized carbon quantum dots (FH-GA-CQDs) were validated for sensing ClO− in living cells and imaging ClO− nude mice. For the nanoprobe, blue emissive glycosidic acid CQDs were chosen as a linker that connected the CQDs to provide an energy donor as well as an anchoring site and green-emission hydrazine hydrate-modified fluorescein (FH) recognition unit and acceptor. The FH-GA-CQDs and their fluorescent test strips were applied for the detection of ClO− in real water examples [157].

Maple leaves derived green CQDs were utilized as a probe for Cesium ion sensing. M-CQDs were effectively used as Cesium sensing probes based on the electron transfer process and has been simultaneously used as a catalyst for glycerol electrooxidation [158]. Park et al. has reported glutathione conjugated CQDs (GSH-CQDs) for sensitive and selective detection of levopods. Levopoda is the prodrug that is converted to dopamine. A fluorescent sensor spike recovery test has also been performed. The detection of levopoda into human serum by spiked and recovery method was also investigated [159]. Figs. 12 and 13
represent the sensitivity and Specificity of levodopa detection by the GSH-CQDs fluorescent sensor respectively. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots prepared via hydrothermal synthesis has been utilized as a naked eye fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of dopamine (DA) [160]. Boronic acid functionalized carbon dots have been for the fluorescent nonenzymatic blood sugar sensing applications [161]. Highly luminescent nitrogen doped carbon dots were employed as turn-on fluorescence probe for selective detection of melamine by Qi Zhuang et al. This fluorescent probe based on iron ions quenched carbon dots as turn on probe for detection of melamine. These nitrogen doped carbon dots can be effectively quenched by Fe3+ and by addition of melamine fluorescent intensity gradually recovered [162]. Methotrexate is chemotherapeutic agent which has been recognized as a folate antagonist to inhibit dihydrofolate reductase, reduce DNA synthesis and decrease cell mitosis and proliferation. This methotrexate is detected via N, S co-doped carbon dots based on inner filter effect [163]. The folic acid is sensed with nitrogen doped carbon dots and salicylic acid as precursor via hydrothermal process with excellent detection limit 28nM [164].

CQDs are more promising due to their small size, and cell membrane penetration capacity. CQD applications in the field of drug delivery and cancer theranostics suffers from a selection of carbon sources to carefully regulate CQD properties and uncontrollable ultrasmall particle size [165]. Cancer cells recognition may be accomplished by using folic acid functionalized CQDs by amide condensation reaction which suggests a good method to improve cell screening and disease diagnosis [166]. Chuyi Han et al. have proposed a novel CQDs-based drug delivery system arginine- silver@copper (Arg-Ag@Cu) using L-arginine as a precursor with duplex metal co-doping (Ag&Cu) for further DOX loading/releasing and synergistic tumor theranostics. This approach of drug delivery significantly suppressed tumor progression and metastasis with negligible side effects [167].

Highly luminescent CQDs were synthesized via citric acid carbonization additionally, modified by transferring. These CQDs were developed for the targeted drug delivery of doxorubicin (Dox) against breast cancer cell line (MCF7) as an anti-breast cancer drug [168]. Fig. 14 shows possible mechanism of CQD-DOX complexes and are efficient for drug delivery for cancer. The CQD-DOX complexes are providing a novel strategy for cancer treatment. Compared to free DOX, the CQD-DOX complexes were significantly more destructive to the adenoid cystic carcinoma cell line (ACC-2), but exhibited lower toxicity to a mouse fibroblast cell line (L929). Confocal microscopy and flow cytometry has confirmed that CQD-DOX complexes increased cancer therapy efficiency through the localization of a much higher quantity of drugs in the nuclei of tumor cells and induced a higher rate of apoptosis in ACC-2 cells, compared to DOX alone [169]. 5-fluorouracil is chemotherapeutic drug used in various types of cancer having variety of side effects and low solubility in water. The Zavareh et al. have reported synthesis of 5 fluorouracil-chitoson-carbon quantum dot apmater (5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt) nanoparticle for specific targeted delivery of 5-FU anti-cancer drug for breast cancer therapy. The cytotoxicity study, flow cytometry, fluorescence microscopy, and Real-Time PCR test results shows the blank nanoparticle was biocompatible and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt revealed effective cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells thus could be applied as a potential nanocarrier in breast cancer treatment [170]. Naproxen non steroidal, anti-inflammatory drug delivered by magneto carbon quantum dots modified with mesoporous silica probe (Mn/CQD/SiO2) can be served as a drug delivery and tracer system [171]. Samimi et al. reported Gemcitabine delivery via nitrogen doped CQDs to a selectin–targeted drug delivery system, in which Quinic acid molecules were attached on the surface of CQDs to facilitate the receptor-mediated uptake of the carrier by cancer cells. Additional, the blood circulation and tissue biodistribution profiles of resulting nitrogen doped-CQDs-quinic acid-gemcitabine were determined following intravenous administration via tail vein of mice [172]. The m-phenylenediamine CQDs (m-CQDs) are used as multifunctional delivery vehicle. These m-CQDs could deliver oligo-hyalurunic acid into the deep cartilage zones and substantially measure sthe factor's effectiveness. M-CQDs could help bioactive factors penetrate the dense structure of cartilage surface layer into deep cartilage zones, substantially increasing their therapeutic effect [173].

This review, discussed the history, structure, top-down as well as bottom-up synthesis approaches of CQDs, and gave a summary of the latest research progress on CQDs based biomedical applications. The optical properties of CQDs such as Photoluminescence mechanism, quantum confinement effect and doping effect are covered. Various methods of CQDs synthesis, different precursors employed and some of the functionalizing agents utilized for biomedical downstream applications have been covered in this review. An attempt to bring up less complicated, less time consumable, lower cytotoxicity, better biocompatibility, and water-soluble CQDs synthesis methods have been made. The high biocompatibility of CQDs makes them suitable for clinical investigations. Recent advances of CQDs based in-vivo, in-vitro bioimaging applications have been discussed. The potential applications of CQDs like biosensing and drug delivery is extensively covered. Though, become distended, research is still required to improve the ability of CQDs. The multiphoton fluorescence mechanism and up-conversion emission are still imprecise, additional research is needed to elucidate this phenomenon. More CQDs based biomedical research should reach to clinical trials as to gain commercial value.
